main_ingredients: glass noodles (mung bean starch cellophane noodles)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of mushroom and glass noodle filling is approximately 100 g, equivalent to the filling used in 4–5 medium dumplings or 2 medium steamed buns.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Mushrooms, shiitake, cooked (FDC ID 169403) and Noodles, chinese, cellophane or long rice (mung beans), dry (FDC ID 169730); values combined using approximate 60:40 mushroom-to-noodle ratio with seasoning adjustment
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite filling. Values estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries for cooked shiitake mushrooms (FDC ID 169403) and dry mung bean cellophane noodles (FDC ID 169730), blended at an approximate 60:40 ratio by weight, with minor seasoning contributions (soy sauce, sesame oil) factored in. Sodium elevated to reflect soy sauce addition. Input JSON values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.9) were cross-checked against USDA component data and retained where consistent.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; mung bean noodles / cellophane noodles analogous entry (GI ~45, low category)
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for mushroom and glass noodle filling as a composite dish. GI of 45 estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for mung bean cellophane noodles (GI ~45, low category). High dietary fibre from shiitake mushrooms (4.5 g/100 g) further moderates glycemic response. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 21.5) / 100 = 9.7 per 100 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 320 mg per 100 g from soy sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should monitor portion size and reduce or substitute soy sauce with a low-sodium alternative, as the 320 mg sodium per 100 g serving contributes meaningfully toward the 1500–2000 mg daily limit recommended by clinical guidelines; the potassium content (280 mg/100 g) from mushrooms provides a partial counterbalancing benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~45) and high dietary fibre (4.5 g/100 g) supporting glycemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI of approximately 45 and high dietary fibre content of 4.5 g per 100 g make this filling suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in controlled portions; however, total carbohydrate load increases substantially when the filling is enclosed in wheat-based dumpling wrappers, which should be accounted for in meal planning.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and beta-glucan fibre from shiitake mushrooms
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This filling is low in saturated fat (approximately 0.4 g/100 g) and contains zero cholesterol, making it a heart-friendly option; shiitake mushrooms provide beta-glucan dietary fibre which has evidence for modest LDL-cholesterol reduction, though sodium from soy sauce should be moderated in individuals with concurrent hypertension and cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mushroom and Glass Noodle Filling (香菇冬粉, Xiānggū dōngfěn) is a traditional Chinese vegetarian filling used in dumplings (Jiaozi) and steamed buns (Baozi). It is prepared by finely dicing rehydrated or fresh shiitake mushrooms and combining them with chopped, pre-soaked glass noodles made from mung bean starch. The mixture is seasoned with light soy sauce, sesame oil, and white pepper, and may include finely chopped carrot or celery for colour and additional nutrition. The filling is not cooked independently; it is enclosed in a dough wrapper and then boiled, steamed, or pan-fried. The resulting texture is moist, chewy, and umami-rich, with the glass noodles absorbing the savoury juices of the mushrooms and seasonings. Nutritionally, the filling provides approximately 115 kcal per 100 g, with 3.2 g protein, 2.5 g fat, and 21.5 g carbohydrate. It is notably high in dietary fibre (4.5 g/100 g) from the shiitake mushrooms, which also contribute potassium, selenium, and B vitamins including niacin and riboflavin. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low category), making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium is moderate at 320 mg/100 g due to soy sauce seasoning. The dish is vegan, dairy-free, and halal-suitable. Culturally, mushrooms symbolise prosperity and longevity in Chinese tradition, and this filling is commonly served during family gatherings and festive meals.
common_user_queries: What is mushroom and glass noodle filling?
Is glass noodle filling good for diabetics?
What are the ingredients in vegetarian jiaozi filling?
GI of mung bean noodle dish
Is shiitake and cellophane noodle filling healthy?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. Nutrient values were estimated by cross-referencing USDA FoodData Central component entries for cooked shiitake mushrooms and dry mung bean cellophane noodles. Input JSON values (ai_generated, confidence 0.9) were retained where consistent with USDA component data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for mung bean cellophane noodles. Vitamin A value in input (150 mcg) appeared inconsistent with a mushroom-dominant vegetarian dish and was adjusted to 15 mcg RAE, consistent with USDA data for cooked shiitake mushrooms and minor carrot contribution.
notes: 1. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: this is a fully plant-based dish containing no animal products; vitamin B12 is naturally zero in this formulation. Confirmed consistent with USDA FoodData Central entries for shiitake mushrooms and mung bean noodles. 2. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a vegan dish with no animal-derived ingredients. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: no hydrogenated fats used in this preparation. 4. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite filling in its prepared state is not reported in USDA FoodData Central for the blended preparation. 5. Vitamin A value from input JSON (150 mcg) was adjusted to 15 mcg RAE as 150 mcg RAE is inconsistent with a mushroom-dominant dish with only optional minor carrot addition; USDA FoodData Central reports approximately 0 mcg RAE for cooked shiitake mushrooms. 6. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, and polyunsaturated fat estimated from USDA component data for sesame oil and shiitake mushrooms; these are approximate values. 7. GI estimated from analogous mung bean cellophane noodle entry in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this composite filling was found in approved sources. 8. The input source (Chinese Academy of Sciences, http://www.cas.cn/) is not on the approved source list; it has been replaced with USDA FoodData Central as the primary nutrient evidence source per Project Veritas v2 source policy.
